---
title: "Lectures 16 & 17: Intergenerational Mobility and the Allocation of Talent"
author: "Graduate Economic History"
date: "Lukas Althoff"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    chakra: '../../shared/remark-latest.min.js'
    css: ['default', 'metropolis', 'metropolis-fonts', '../../shared/my-css.css']
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r, setup, include = F}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(
  comment = "#>",
  fig.align = "center",
  fig.height = 7,
  fig.width = 10.5,
  warning = F,
  message = F
)
```

class: agenda

# Agenda

<ul class="agenda-list">
<li class="current">Intergenerational Mobility</li>
<li class="upcoming">Allocation of Talent</li>
</ul>

---

# **Motivation:** The Land of Opportunity

.center-content[
- How did the US become a land of opportunity?

- Limited evidence on drivers of mobility, especially role of human capital

- Past evidence focused on men: father's income $\leftrightarrow$ son's income

  - *Reason 1:* Income as primary measure of parental background

  - *Reason 2:* Lack of intergenerational datasets that include women
]

---

# **Historical anecdotes**

.center-content[
1. **Abraham Lincoln** (1809-1865): US President
  - (Step-)mother supported his education; father (illiterate farmer) disinterested in education

2. **Thomas Edison** (1847-1931): American inventor
  - Mother (trained teacher) home-educated him; father had no formal schooling

3. **Katherine Johnson** (1918--2020): NASA mathematician
  - Mother was a teacher; father a janitor (parents moved for school access amid Jim Crow)
  - Own daughter also became NASA mathematician
]

---

# **Preview of results:** Schools as a driver of high mobility

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '50%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/mobility_shareinschool_1870vs1930.png")
```
]

---

# **Historical context:** Shift from home to school education

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '50%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/schoolaccess_evolution.png")
```
]

---

# **This paper:** Intergenerational mobility & maternal human capital

.center-content[
- **Methods:** Measuring intergenerational mobility with multiple inputs

  - Measure mobility as variation unexplained by parental background $(1-R^2)$

  - Decompose predictive power of maternal vs. paternal human capital

  - Address data limitations with Gaussian copula method *<span class="gray">Fan et al. (2017)</span>*

- **Data:** Representative panel tracing both men & women (1850-1950)

  - 186 million linked census records covering 42 million Americans

  - Overcome marriage name changes using SSA administrative data

- **Results:** Maternal human capital & mass schooling shaped mobility

  - Mothers more predictive of children's literacy-based HC, esp. when schooling limited

  $\rightarrow$ Incl. mothers alters conclusions about trends & geography of mobility

  - Human capital mobility rose sharply (0.3 to 0.65) as schooling expanded

  - Distinct mobility patterns for Black Americans post-slavery & during Jim Crow
]

---

# **Literature**

.center-content[
1. **Measuring US intergenerational mobility**

  - Father-child correlations *<span class="gray">Abramitzky et al. 2021, Ward 2023, Olivetti & Paserman (2015)</span>*

  - Parents' average status *<span class="gray">Chetty et al. 2014, Card et al. (2022)</span>*

  - **This paper:** Directly estimates mothers' distinct role in shaping mobility

2. **Women's contributions to long-run economic development**

  - Labor force participation mid-20th century *<span class="gray">Goldin 1977, 1990, 2006, Fern\'andez et al. (2004)</span>*

  - Time spent in home production *<span class="gray">Greenwood et al. 2005, Ramey 2009, Ngai et al. (2024)</span>*

  - **This paper:** Quantifies key "hidden" contribution via human capital transmission

3. **Datasets including women in historical mobility studies**

  - Automated record linkage for men *<span class="gray">Abramitzky et al. (2020)</span>*

  - Women via vital records or crowd-sourced family trees *<span class="gray">Craig et al. 2019, Buckles et al. (2023)</span>*

  - **This paper:** Uses historical administrative data for both scale & representativeness
]

---
class: inverse, middle, center

# Intergenerational Datasets

---

# **Data (childhood):** Census cross-sections of children & parents

.center-content[
- Full-count census data from 1850 to 1950

  - Identifies family relationships within households

  $\rightarrow$ observe parents & children jointly

  - Children (13-16): Measure literacy (1850-1930) and years of schooling (1940-1950)

  - Parents: Measure literacy and years of schooling

- Advantages:

  - Full US population

  - Can directly analyze transmission of human capital during childhood

- Limitations:

  - Cannot observe children's outcomes once they reach adulthood

  - Next: New intergenerational dataset
]

---

# **Data (adulthood):** Historical administrative data (SSA)

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '94%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/ssn_app_filledin_type_color.png")
```
]

---
name: linkingprocess

# **Data (adulthood):** Linking women despite name changes

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '95%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/linking_process_6.png")
```
]

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#multistagelinking" class="btn">Multi-stage linking process</a></div>

---
name: representativeness_across_years

# **Data (adulthood):** Representativeness & coverage (1850-1950)

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '46%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_across_years.png")
```
]

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#coveragecensus" class="btn">Coverage</a> <a href="#rep1940" class="btn">Rep. 1910–1940</a> <a href="#rep1910" class="btn">Rep. 1880–1910</a> <a href="#rep1880" class="btn">Rep. 1850–1880</a> <a href="#linkrates" class="btn">Linking rates</a> <a href="#linkingoverlap" class="btn">Agreement rates</a> <a href="#errorrates" class="btn">Error rates</a></div>

---
class: inverse, middle, center

# Measuring Mobility with Multiple Parental Inputs

---

# **Strategy:** Simple model of rank mobility with multiple inputs

.center-content[
$$\text{rank}(Y_{i}) = \alpha + \boldsymbol{\beta}_{1} \textbf{rank}\left(\boldsymbol{Y}^{\text{father}}_{i}\right) + \boldsymbol{\beta}_{2} \textbf{rank}\left(\boldsymbol{Y}^{\text{mother}}_{i}\right) + \varepsilon_{i}$$

- $Y_i^j$: $i$'s outcome (e.g., human capital)

- **New:** Explicitly incorporates multiple parental inputs
]

---
name: r2tocoefficient

# **Strategy:** Measuring mobility via $R^2$

.center-content[
$$R^2 = \frac{\text{Variance in child outcomes explained by parents}}{\text{Variance in child outcomes}}$$

- **Comparability:** Closely tied to parent-child coefficient $\beta$

  - $R^2 = \left(\beta \cdot \frac{\sigma_X}{\sigma_Y}\right)^2$

  - One-to-one mapping with rank-rank coefficient: $R^2 = \beta^2$

- **Multiple parental inputs**

- **Intuitive interpretation**
]

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#details_r2tocoefficient" class="btn">Details: R² ↔ β</a></div>

---
class: inverse, middle, center

# Human Capital Mobility

---

# **Strategy:** Rank-rank human capital mobility

.center-content[
$$HC^{\text{child}}_{i} = \alpha_{g} + \beta_{f} \cdot HC^{\text{father}}_{i} + \beta_{m} \cdot HC^{\text{mother}}_{i} + \varepsilon_{i}$$

- We only observe literacy as a binary proxy of (latent) human capital.

- **Solution:** Gaussian copula method *<span class="gray">Fan et al. (2017)</span>*
]

---
name: illustratingsplv

# **Strategy:** Identification of rank mobility despite data limitations

.center-content[
- **Goal:** Estimate mobility (1-$R^2$) via rank($Y_i$) = $\alpha$ + $\beta \cdot$ rank($X_i$) + $\epsilon_i$

- **Challenge:** Only binary indicators observed: $Y^*_i = \mathbb{1}[Y_i > \delta_y]$ and $X^*_i = \mathbb{1}[X_i > \delta_x]$

  $\rightarrow$ Many possible rank correlations compatible with binary data

- **Solution:** Gaussian copula method *<span class="gray">Fan et al. (2017)</span>*

  $\rightarrow$ Allows time-varying marginal distr. $\Rightarrow$ rising literacy does not inflate mobility

- **Validation:** Method performs well under various conditions

  - Alternative methods (Kendall's $\tau$, normalized $R^2$) confirm same patterns

  - Simulation: Accurately recovers true rank mobility from binary data

  - 1930-1940 comparison: High correlation (0.85) across states, sex, and race

  - Modern data (NLSY79, PSID): Robust to extreme cutoffs in dichotomization
]

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#assumptionlatent" class="btn">Assumption</a> <a href="#illustratingsplv_details" class="btn">Method</a> <a href="#illustratingsplv_pic" class="btn">Visual</a> <a href="#validationlatent2" class="btn">Simulation</a> <a href="#valid19301940" class="btn">1930–1940</a> <a href="#validationlatent_nlsy" class="btn">NLSY (1/2)</a> <a href="#validationlatent_nlsy2" class="btn">NLSY (2/2)</a> <a href="#validationlatent" class="btn">Historical 1940</a> <a href="#validkendall" class="btn">Kendall's τ</a> <a href="#validnormr2" class="btn">Norm. R²</a> <a href="#validmeaserror" class="btn">Meas. error</a></div>

---

# **Results:** Human capital mobility rises over US history

.pull-left[
**All**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/figure3_panel_a_with_cis_all_years.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**By group**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/figure3_panel_b_with_cis_all_years.png")
```
]

.footnote[.small[Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals shown. All available census waves included.]]

---

# **Strategy:** Assessing drivers of changing mobility

.center-content[
- **Decomposition of** $\boldsymbol{R^2}$ **into components**

$$R^2 = \beta_m^2 + \beta_f^2 + 2\beta_m \beta_f\hat{\rho}_{m,f}$$

1. **Maternal contribution:** $\beta_m^2$ (mothers' predictive power)

2. **Paternal contribution:** $\beta_f^2$ (fathers' predictive power)

3. **Assortative mating:** $2\beta_m \beta_f\hat{\rho}_{m,f}$ (where $\hat{\rho}_{m,f}$ is correlation between parents)

- **Application:** Counterfactual analysis by holding parameters constant
]

---

# **Results:** Changing role of mothers drives rising mobility

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/latgaussbin2_counterfactual_r2_betas_xsection.png")
```
]

---

# **Strategy:** Separating contribution of mothers vs. fathers to $R^2$

.center-content[
- **Shapley-Owen decomposition of** $\boldsymbol{R^2}$ *<span class="gray">Shapley 1953; Owen 1977</span>*

- **Unique advantages** of this method *<span class="gray">Young 1985</span>*

1. **Additivity:** Individual contributions add up to the total $R^2$

2. **Equal treatment:** Perfectly substitutable regressors receive equal values

3. **Monotonicity:** More predictive regressors receive larger values
]

---

# **Results:** Mothers disproportionally predict child's human capital

.pull-left[
**All**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/parenthumancap_slv_xsection_lit_literate_cohort_sharemom_latgaussbin.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**By group**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/parenthumancap_slv_xsection_lit_literate_cohort_bygroup_sharemom_latgaussbin.png")
```
]

---
name: famtype_mother

# **Results:** Mothers' predictive power across family types

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/parenthumancap_xsection_latgaussbin_familystatus1.png")
```

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#famtype_father" class="btn">Fathers</a></div>

.footnote[.small[Results are for 1900 cohort, based on single input model that excludes fathers.]]

---

# **Results:** Geography of human capital mobility after incorporating mothers

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '23%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/change_r2_1870_1930_bystate.png")
```
]

---

# Takeaways: Human Capital Mobility

.center-content[
**Surging human capital mobility in two episodes**

1. **Black:** After end of slavery
2. **White:** Early 1900s ($\sim$ school attendance becomes universal)

**Maternal human capital** key predictor of child HC

- **Mother's human capital** more predictive than father's
- **Female & Black children** have stronger maternal link
]

---
class: inverse, middle, center

# Rising Human Capital Mobility & Mass Schooling

---

# **Results:** Schools as a driver of high mobility

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '48%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/mobility_shareinschool_1870vs1930.png")
```
]

---

# **Results:** Human capital mobility & school access

.pull-left[
**Mothers**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/geo_school_mom_latgaussbin.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Fathers**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/geo_school_pop_latgaussbin.png")
```
]

.footnote[.small[Estimates separate by sex, race, state.]]

---
name: schoolaccess_table

# **Results:** Human capital mobility & *two different measures* of school access

.center-content[
|  | $\phi_{\text{Mother}}$ | $\phi_{\text{Father}}$ | $\frac{\phi_{\text{Mother}}}{R^2}$ | $\phi_{\text{Mother}}$ | $\phi_{\text{Father}}$ | $\frac{\phi_{\text{Mother}}}{R^2}$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Baseline measure of school access** | -0.18*** | 0.04 | -0.20*** |  |  |  |
|  | (0.03) | (0.05) | (0.03) |  |  |  |
| **Refined measure of school access** |  |  |  | -0.47*** | 0.15 | -0.58*** |
| *(accounts for attendance, term lengths, etc.)* |  |  |  | (0.08) | (0.11) | (0.10) |
| R$^2$ | 0.39 | 0.02 | 0.51 | 0.37 | 0.04 | 0.57 |
| Observations | 133 | 133 | 133 | 128 | 128 | 128 |
]

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#schoolquality" class="btn">School quality (1/2)</a> <a href="#schoolquality2" class="btn">School quality (2/2)</a></div>

---

# **Strategy:** Isolating causal impact of schooling on maternal influence

.center-content[
$$\phi^{\text{Mother}}_{s,r,yob} = \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{inschool}_{s,r,yob} + \gamma_{yob} + \varepsilon_{s,r,yob}$$

- **Identification strategy**

  - Outcome: Mother's contribution to intergenerational persistence ($\phi^{\text{Mother}}$)

  - Endogenous variable: Share of children in school by state-race ($\text{inschool}_{s,r,yob}$)

  - Exploit staggered implementation of compulsory schooling laws across states

  - Instrument: Number of years child exposed to compulsory schooling
]

---

# **Results:** Causal evidence from mandatory schooling legislation

.center-content[
| | OLS | IV | OLS | IV |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **IV: Schooling via compulsory schooling laws** | -0.23*** | -0.92*** | -0.73*** | -0.92*** |
| | (0.04) | (0.22) | (0.18) | (0.22) |
| Cohort Fixed Effects | Y | Y | Y | Y |
| Sample restricted to 1920-1940 | N | N | Y | Y |
| F-statistic | -- | 35.52 | -- | 35.39 |
| R$^2$ | 0.47 | -- | 0.38 | -- |
| Observations | 1,049 | 1,049 | 465 | 465 |
]

---

# Takeaways: Schools and Mobility

.center-content[
1. **School access** $\leftrightarrow$ **human capital mobility** (time, space, sex, race)

  **With school access, mothers' contribution converges w/ fathers'**

2. **Schooling** $\Rightarrow$ **human capital mobility** via evidence from staggered mandatory school laws
]

---
class: inverse, middle, center

# Income Mobility & Human Capital Mobility Rise Together

---

# **Strategy:** Measuring income mobility incorporating human capital

.center-content[
$$\text{rank}(\text{inc}_i) = \alpha + \beta_p\text{rank}(\text{inc}^{\text{parents}}_i) + \beta_m\text{rank}(h^{\text{mother}}_i) + \beta_f\text{rank}(h^{\text{father}}_i) + \varepsilon_i$$

- **Method:** Gaussian copula method to handle binary proxies (literacy)

- **Data:** Applied to our new representative panel dataset
]

---
name: incomemobresults

# **Results:** Income mobility rises in tandem with human capital mobility

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/parentalbackground_lido_cohort_latgaussbin_new2.png")
```
]

<div class="btn-row"><a href="#altincomemeasures" class="btn">Alt. income measures</a> <a href="#litcomparison" class="btn">Lit. comparison</a></div>

---

# **Results:** Changing role of mothers sole driver of rising mobility ($\beta_m$)

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/latgaussbin2_counterfactual_r2_betas_new.png")
```
]

---

# **Conclusion**

.center-content[
- Mothers' human capital was key predictor of children's outcomes before mass schooling

  **→ Including mothers alters key conclusions** *(e.g., South as least mobile region)*

- Mobility in human capital and income surged from the 19th to the 20th century

  **→ US was not born as country of opportunity**

- Mass schooling replaced home education, reducing reliance on maternal human capital

  **→ High mobility is not guaranteed---depends on public provision of schooling**

  **→ Quality schooling especially benefits children of less-educated parents**
]

---
class: agenda

# Agenda

<ul class="agenda-list">
<li class="done">Intergenerational Mobility</li>
<li class="current">Allocation of Talent</li>
</ul>

---

# Allocation of Talent

.center-content[
- Barriers by race, gender, class, ... distort *whose* talents are used

- This creates a gap between *potential* vs. *realized* talent allocation

- Why the gap matters:
  - **Fairness**: Effort and ability, not parental background or identity orthogonal to those
  - **Growth**: Misallocation leaves ideas undiscovered & output unrealized
  - **Direction**: Narrow inventor pool skews R&D toward problems salient to dominant group

- Removing barriers has potential to reallocate talent, increasing **equity** and **output**
]

---

# Who Becomes an Inventor? <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
- Research Question
  - Nature (inherent ability) vs. nurture (environment) in innovation career choice
  - Who becomes an inventor in the United States today?

- Key Data
  - 1.2 million inventors linked to tax records
  - Track inventors from birth to adulthood

- Main Finding
  - Children from top 1% income families are **10x more likely** to become inventors
  - Large gaps persist even among children with similar math test scores
  - Evidence suggests environment, not just ability, matters
]

---

# Identifying Causal Effects of Exposure <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
- Methodological Innovation:
  - Exploit variation across specific technology classes
  - Compare children's patent classes to parents/neighbors' patent classes
  - $\Rightarrow$ Innate ability unlikely to vary across narrow tech classes

- Key Results:
  - Children of inventors patent in their parent's exact tech class ~5x more often than in the next-closest class
  - Exposure effects are gender-specific: girls more influenced by female inventors in the same class
  - Moving from a 25th- to 75th-percentile commuting zone (e.g., New Orleans → Austin) raises a child's invention probability by 37%

- Evidence for causal exposure effects, not just selection
]

---

# Lost Einsteins <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
```{r, echo = F, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/inventor_inc.png")
```
]

---

# Lost Einsteins <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
```{r, echo = F, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/inventor_race.png")
```
]

---

# Lost Einsteins <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
```{r, echo = F, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/inventor_sex.png")
```
]

---

# Lost Einsteins <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
```{r, echo = F, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/inventor_inc_sexrace.png")
```
]

---

# Lost Einsteins <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
```{r, echo = F, out.width = '52%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/inventor_class.png")
```

.footnote[.small[Genetic ability is unlikely to explain this pattern. A child shouldn't have genes that specifically make them better at inventing "pulse amplifiers" rather than "antenna oscillators"---these are very similar technological categories that would require similar skills and knowledge.]]
]

---

# Lost Einsteins <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
```{r, echo = F, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/inventor_geo.png")
```
]

---

# Lost Einsteins and Policy Implications <br> .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
- **"Lost Einsteins"**
  - Under-represented inventors have similar earnings and citations as others
  - $\Rightarrow$ Women/minorities/low-income who don't invent aren't marginal talents

- Counterfactual
  - If women, minorities, and low-income children invented at same rate as white men from high-income families
  - $\Rightarrow$ **4x more inventors** in America

- Policy
  - Focus on increasing exposure to innovation for under-represented groups
  - May have larger impact than traditional financial incentives
]

---

# Taking a Step Back: Mechanisms for Lost Einsteins

.center-content[
- Role-model "exposure" elasticities .small[<span class="gray">Bell et al. (2019)</span>]
  - Children exposed to inventors are more likely to invent
  - Gender and race-specific exposure effects strongest

- Credit & information frictions
  - Costly education, lack of exposure to STEM careers
  - Limited networks for mentorship and financing

- Structural discrimination
  - School quality differentials
  - Geographic segregation limiting opportunity
  - Social capital .small[<span class="gray">Chetty et al. (2022)</span>]
]

---

# Climbing the ivory tower: talent in academic science

.center-content[
- Human capital drives science <span class="gray">Waldinger (2016)</span>
  - Nazi dismissals of (mostly Jewish) scientists + Allied bombing of German universities
  - 10% loss of researchers → 0.2 SD drop in dept. output, persisting through 1980
  - 10% loss of facilities → 0.05 SD drop, recovering by ~1961
  - Star scientists drive disproportionate output; their loss degrades subsequent hires

- "Hidden stars" <span class="gray">Hager, Schwarz & Waldinger (2024)</span>
  - First citation database created quasi-random visibility of researchers' output
  - Reduced information frictions in hiring → assortative matching of scientists to departments
  - Highly cited researchers from lower-ranked departments and minority groups gained most

- Socio-economic background still shapes who climbs <span class="gray">Abramitzky, Greska, Perez, Price, Schwarz & Waldinger (2024)</span>
  - Academics from poorer backgrounds under-represented for seven decades; more at elite unis
  - Father's occupation predicts professors' field of research $\Rightarrow$ steers research direction
  - Poorer-background academics introduce more novel concepts but  fewer citations, awards
]

---

# Macroeconomic Cost of Talent Misallocation <br> .small[<span class="gray">Hsieh et al. (2019)</span>]

.center-content[
- Occupational Convergence (1960-2010):
  - 1960: 94% of doctors/lawyers were white men
  - 2010: Only 62% of doctors/lawyers were white men
  - Assumption: Innate talent distribution unchanging across groups

- Three Sources of Misallocation:
  - Labor market discrimination (wage-productivity wedges)
  - Barriers to human capital formation
  - Differences in preferences/social norms

- Macroeconomic Impact:
  - **20-40% of growth in US GDP per worker** (1960-2010) due to declining talent-allocation barriers
  - Mostly from reduced barriers to human capital formation; less from decl. labor market discrim.
]

---

# Who Innovates $\rightarrow$ What Gets Innovated

.center-content[
- Directed technical change: inventors' identities steer R&D topics <span class="gray">Acemoglu (2002)</span>
  - Inventors focus on problems they personally understand or value
  - Market size matters, but inventor demographics also shape focus

- Example facts:
  - All-female inventor teams 35% more likely than all-male teams to focus on women's health <span class="gray">Koning, Samila & Ferguson (2021)</span>
  - Black NIH applicants disproportionately propose work on community-level interventions and health disparities — topics with lower funding rates <span class="gray">Hoppe et al. (2019)</span>
  - Under-represented groups introduce more novel scientific concepts but get less credit <span class="gray">Hofstra et al. (2020)</span>
]

---
class: inverse, middle, center

# Appendix

---

# **Background:** The history of the Social Security Number

.center-content[
- **Social Security Number** was introduced in 1936

- By 1937, 37M people have SSN (28% of pop.)
  - Initially only for tracking earnings of working population
  - Expanded substantially in later years (e.g. for taxes, Medicare)

- Increasingly representative
  - By 1971, SSNs obtained by 107M men & 93M women
  - More representative due to inclusion of applicant's parents
]

---

# **Data:** Inputs to our panel

.center-content[
**SSN Applications**

- Near-universe of SSN applicants who died before 2007
- Includes name, age, race, state and city of birth, parents' maiden names, SSN

**Census**

- Restricted-access Censuses 1850-1940
]

---

# **Data:** Step 1 of panel construction

.center-content[
**SSN Applications** $\longrightarrow$ link to each available Census decade (1850, 1860, ..., 1930, 1940)

- One SSN application record connects directly to a person's appearance in each historical Census
- Provides the spine of the panel
]

---

# **Data:** Step 2 of panel construction

.center-content[
After linking SSN $\longleftrightarrow$ Census, link Census records to one another using SSN-based matches

- Bridges consecutive Census decades for both applicants and their parents
- Allows tracking individuals (and family structure) across the 1850-1940 panel
]

---
name: multistagelinking

# **Data:** Multi-stage linking process

.center-content[
1. **Applicant SSN** $\longleftrightarrow$ **census (1850-1940)**

  - Find unique match via applicant *and* parent names, year & state of birth, race, sex

  - Progressively relax set of criteria to literature standard *<span class="gray">Abramitzky et al. (2020)</span>*

2. **Parent SSN** $\longleftrightarrow$ **census**

  - Direct link from parent to census not possible due to lack of birth details

  - If child is linked to parent household, parents can be assigned (pot. synthetic) SSN

3. **Census** $\longleftrightarrow$ **census**

  - Link census records using SSNs for all possible pairs 1850-1940
]

<a href="#linkingprocess" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---

# **Data:** New "Waterfall" linking algorithm

.center-content[
1. Link SSNs to census starting with maximum information set (gradually reduced)
  - Min. information set: first/**middle**/last name, **race**, year/state of birth, **mother's**/**father's first name**, **women's marital status**
  - If no unique match, try again with subset of blocking variables
  - Min. information set: literature standard (excl. additional variables, *Abramitzky et al. 2021*)

2. After identifying SSN applicant's census records, match parents if present

3. Find parents across census decades using Waterfall algorithm

4. Link census decades using SSN (artificial SSN for parents who never applied)
]

---
name: details_r2tocoefficient

# **Strategy:** Relationship between $R^2$ and parent-child coefficient $\beta$

.center-content[
In a linear regression with a single explanatory variable, $y_i = \alpha + \beta x_i + \varepsilon_i$, the coefficient $\beta$ and the $R^2$ are defined as follows:

$$\beta = \text{cor}(x, y) \cdot \frac{\text{sd}(y)}{\text{sd}(x)}$$

$$R^2 = (\text{cor}(x, y))^2 = \left(\beta \cdot \frac{\text{sd}(x)}{\text{sd}(y)}\right)^2$$

By their very nature, percentile ranked outcomes share the same distribution. Therefore, if both $y$ and $x$ are outcomes in percentile ranks, we have $\text{sd}(y)=\text{sd}(x)$ and thus

$$R^2=\beta^2$$
]

<a href="#r2tocoefficient" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: coveragecensus

# **Data (adulthood):** Coverage of our new panel

.center-content[
**Fraction of US Population Linked in Our New Panel**

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '80%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/numident_to_census_pct_all.png")
```
]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: rep1880

# **Data:** Representativeness of our new panel (1850-1880)

.pull-left[
**Women (1850-1880)**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_1850_1880_wom.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Men (1850-1880)**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_1850_1880_men.png")
```
]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: rep1910

# **Data:** Representativeness of our new panel (1880-1910)

.pull-left[
**Women (1880-1910)**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_1880_1910_wom.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Men (1880-1910)**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_1880_1910_men.png")
```
]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: rep1940

# **Data (adulthood):** Balance of long-run linked sample (1910-1940)

.pull-left[
**Women**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_1910_1940_wom.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Men**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/representativeness_1910_1940_men.png")
```
]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: linkrates

# **Data:** High linking rates for our new panel

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/linking_rate_comparison.png")
```
]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: linkingoverlap

# **Data:** High agreement rates on specific observations linked

.pull-left[
**Men & women**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/linking_agreement_rates.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Women with name changes**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/linking_agreement_rates_marwom.png")
```
]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---

# **Strategy:** Example of Shapley-Owen Decomposition of $R^2$

.center-content[
<table style="margin: 0 auto 1.5em;">
<thead>
<tr><th></th><th colspan="2">Before</th><th colspan="2">After</th><th>Marginal contribution</th></tr>
<tr><th></th><th>Regression inputs</th><th>R²</th><th>Regression inputs</th><th>R²</th><th></th></tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td></td><td>∅</td><td>0</td><td>Mother</td><td>0.15</td><td>0.15 − 0 = 0.15</td></tr>
<tr><td></td><td>Father</td><td>0.05</td><td>Father & Mother</td><td>0.25</td><td>0.25 − 0.05 = 0.20</td></tr>
</tbody>
</table>

$\Rightarrow$ Mother's total contribution $=\frac{1}{2}\left(0.15+0.2\right)=0.175$

Can be generalized to include many inputs (incl. interaction effects, controls)
]

---
name: assumptionlatent

# **Strategy:** Gaussian copula assumption

.center-content[
**Main assumption:** Parental & child outcomes drawn from a joint Gaussian copula

- That is, we assume that there exists a set of (unknown) monotonically increasing transformations $f_{\text{c}},f_{\text{m}},f_{\text{f}}$ such that

$$\left\{f_{\text{c}}\left(y^{\text{child}}_{i}\right),f_{\text{m}}\left(y^{\text{mother}}_{i}\right),f_{\text{f}}\left(y^{\text{father}}_{i}\right)\right\}^T \sim \mathcal{N}\left(0,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\right) \quad\text{ w/ } \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \mathbb{1}$$

- Gaussian copula increasingly popular in statistics due to flexibility and performance *<span class="gray">Liu et al. (2009); 2012; Zue & Zou (2012)</span>*

- Includes wide range of marginal distributions (incl. skewed, multi-modal, etc.)
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: illustratingsplv_details

# **Strategy:** Illustrating the Gaussian copula method

.center-content[
1. Estimate Kendall's $\tau$ from binary data:

$$\hat{\tau} = \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \sum_{1 \leq i < i' \leq n} (X^*_{i} - X^*_{i'})(Y^*_{i} - Y^*_{i'})$$

2. Assume $(X_i,Y_i)\sim$ **Gaussian copula** $\Rightarrow$ Latent correlation $\rho$ identified:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\tau}] = 2\left[\Phi_2(\Delta_X, \Delta_Y, \rho) - \Phi(\Delta_X)\Phi(\Delta_Y)\right]$$

3. Convert $\hat\rho$ to rank correlation $\hat\rho_r$ and $R^2$:

$$\hat\rho_r = \frac{6}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{\hat\rho}{2}\right) \qquad\qquad R^2 = \hat\rho_r^2$$
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: validationlatent2

# **Validation:** Simulation from Gaussian copula distribution

.pull-left[
Simulate parent & child ranks based on empirical distribution of literacy correlation by decade

1. **Truth** via simulated ranks
2. **Our method** via literacy dummies
3. **OLS** (standard) via literacy dummies
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '95%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/latgaussbin_sim_validation.png")
```
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: valid19301940

# **Validation:** Mobility via binary literacy ('30) vs. continuous educ. ('40)

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '50%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/mobility_litvseduc_1930vs1940_new.png")
```

.footnote[.small[Estimates by state, sex, race.]]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: illustratingsplv_pic

# **Strategy:** Illustrating the Gaussian copula method

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/illustrating_fan_notext.png")
```
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: validationlatent_nlsy

# **Validation:** Modern survey data on cognitive tests scores (1/2)

.pull-left[
AFQT & other cognitive test scores from NLSY

- **Benchmark:** True rank-rank $R^2$
- **Our method:** Rank-rank $R^2$ using **arbitrarily dichotomized data**
]

.pull-right[
**Income mobility**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '95%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/incomemobility_nlsy79_latgaussbin_vs_actual.png")
```
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: validationlatent_nlsy2

# **Validation:** Modern survey data on cognitive tests scores (2/2)

.pull-left[
AFQT & other cognitive test scores from NLSY

- **Benchmark:** True rank-rank $R^2$
- **Our method:** Rank-rank $R^2$ using **arbitrarily dichotomized data**
]

.pull-right[
**Human capital mobility**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '95%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/parentchild_hc_nlsy79_latgaussbin_vs_actual_q_alltests.png")
```
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: validationlatent

# **Validation:** Historical census data on years of education

.pull-left[
Years of education from 1940 census

- **Benchmark:** True rank-rank $R^2$
- **Our method:** Rank-rank $R^2$ using **arbitrarily dichotomized data**
]

.pull-right[
**Educational mobility by US state**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '95%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/latgaussbin_explore1940.png")
```
]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: famtype_father

# **Results:** Fathers' predictive power across family types

**Father's** $\rightarrow$ **child's (latent) human capital rank**

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '55%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/parenthumancap_xsection_latgaussbin_familystatus2.png")
```

<a href="#famtype_mother" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: errorrates

# **Data:** Error rates across linking datasets

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/error_rates_all.png")
```

.footnote[.small[Error rates based on "ground truth" variables not used for matching: immigration year, literacy consistency, parental birthplace.]]

<a href="#representativeness_across_years" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>
]

---
name: validkendall

# **Validation:** Kendall's $\tau$ (no Gaussian copula assumption)

.center-content[
<img src="files/parenthumancap_xsection_kendallstau_mom.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />
<img src="files/parenthumancap_xsection_kendallstau_mom_wht.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />
<img src="files/parenthumancap_xsection_kendallstau_mom_blk.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />

.footnote[.small[Kendall's $\tau$ of children's and mothers' literacy over time, compared to Gaussian copula estimates. Left: All; Middle: White Americans; Right: Black Americans.]]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>
]

---
name: validnormr2

# **Validation:** Normalized $R^2$ <br> .small[<span class="gray">Nekoei & Sinn (2021)</span>]

.center-content[
<img src="files/parenthumancap_xsection_mobility_r2norm.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />
<img src="files/parenthumancap_xsection_mobility_r2norm_wht.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />
<img src="files/parenthumancap_xsection_mobility_r2norm_blk.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />

.footnote[.small[Normalized $R^2$ using cohort-specific bounds of marginal distribution, compared to Gaussian copula $R^2$. Left: All; Middle: White Americans; Right: Black Americans.]]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>
]

---
name: validmeaserror

# **Validation:** Robustness to measurement error

.pull-left[
**Fathers only**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/father_mobility_weighted.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Both parents**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/family_mobility_weighted.png")
```
]

.footnote[.small[Restricted sample: parents with consistent literacy across linked census waves (Ward '23).]]

<a href="#illustratingsplv" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: litcomparison

# **Results:** Benchmarking with previous literature

.center-content[
<img src="files/lit_comp_all_new.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />
<img src="files/lit_comp_all_new_whtmen.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />
<img src="files/lit_comp_all_new_blkmen.png" width="32%" style="display:inline-block" />

.footnote[.small[Comparison with Ward '23, Jácome et al. '25, Buckles et al. '23. Converting $\beta$ to $1-R^2$ via $1-\beta^2$. Left: All; Middle: White men; Right: Black men.]]

<a href="#incomemobresults" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>
]

---
name: schoolquality

# **Results:** School quality by race and region (1/2)

.pull-left[
**Student-teacher ratio**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '75%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/census_abstract_student_teacher_ratio.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Daily school attendance**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '75%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/census_abstract_attendance.png")
```
]

.footnote[.small[Census statistical abstracts (1870-1920). School resources explain 40--80% of variation in mobility.]]

<a href="#schoolaccess_table" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: schoolquality2

# **Results:** School quality by race and region (2/2)

.pull-left[
**School expenditures**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '75%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/census_abstract_total_spending.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
**Teacher salaries**
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '75%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/census_abstract_salary_spending.png")
```
]

.footnote[.small[Census statistical abstracts (1870-1920). School resources explain 40--80% of variation in mobility.]]

<a href="#schoolaccess_table" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>

---
name: altincomemeasures

# **Results:** Income mobility using alternative income measures

.center-content[
```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = '60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("files/income_measures_mobility_inclsong.png")
```

.footnote[.small[LIDO scores (1950 census), time-varying Song scores (decade-specific literacy), and occscores.]]

<a href="#incomemobresults" class="btn btn-back">Back</a>
]
